profileEditor.config.reg.cpraDesc: California 2023. Opt-out for sale/sharing; opt-in for sensitive data. GPC honored.
profileEditor.config.reg.dpdpaDesc: India 2023. Opt-in like GDPR. GPC not recognised. Data Fiduciary notice required.

Profiles: {{names}}.

Saving will update cookie definitions for all profiles using this template. Continue?
cookieTemplates.editor.legalBasis.mandat: Mandatory (Strictly Necessary): The cookie is always granted — the visitor cannot turn it off. Use only for cookies genuinely required for the site to function (session tokens, CSRF, load balancing).
"Strictly necessary" cookies are exempt from consent. Do not mark analytics or advertising cookies as Mandatory.
cookieTemplates.editor.legalBasis.exampl: Consent: analytics_storage · ad_storage · Legitimate Interest: server-side analytics · Mandatory: session · csrf_token · load_balancer_id

howItWorks.title: How Consenti Works
howItWorks.intro: Consenti is an open-source, GDPR-compliant Consent Management Platform. It handles cookie consent collection, storage, and reporting for your websites. This guide walks you through the main workflow and describes every section of the dashboard.
howItWorks.workflow.heading: Core Workflow
howItWorks.workflow.step1.title: Define Cookie Categories
howItWorks.workflow.step1.desc: Go to Consent Banners → Cookie Templates and create a template that lists every cookie category your site uses (e.g. analytics, marketing). Set the legal basis and expiry for each.
howItWorks.workflow.step2.title: Design the Consent UI
howItWorks.workflow.step2.desc: Go to Consent Banners → UI Templates and configure the visual layout of your banner and preference modal — button labels, colours, layout style, and category display.
howItWorks.workflow.step3.title: Create a Consent Profile
howItWorks.workflow.step3.desc: Go to Consent Banners → Profiles and combine a Cookie Template with a UI Template. Add translated text for every locale you support, enable the required regulations (GDPR, CCPA, DPDPA), and publish.
howItWorks.workflow.step4.title: Embed the Widget
howItWorks.workflow.step4.desc: Copy your API key from API → Config and add the Consenti script tag to your website. The widget fetches your published profile and shows the consent banner automatically.
howItWorks.workflow.step5.title: Monitor and Audit
howItWorks.workflow.step5.desc: Use the Consents and Visitors pages to review live consent records. Use the Audit Log for a tamper-evident history of all administrative actions for compliance evidence.
howItWorks.features.heading: Dashboard Features
howItWorks.features.dashboard.title: Dashboard
howItWorks.features.dashboard.desc: The home screen shows real-time KPIs — total consents, acceptance rate, rejection rate, and a time-series chart. Use it to spot trends and gauge the impact of banner changes.
howItWorks.features.cookieTemplates.desc: Reusable lists of cookie categories. Each category has an ID, legal basis (consent, legitimate interest, necessary), GPC signal handling, expiry, and optional TCF vendor or CPRA category mapping.
howItWorks.features.uiTemplates.title: UI Templates
howItWorks.features.uiTemplates.desc: Controls the look and feel of the consent banner, GPC notice, and preference modal. Configure button sets, heading tags, overlay opacity, and category display order.
howItWorks.features.profiles.title: Profiles
howItWorks.features.profiles.desc: A Profile is the deployable unit. It binds a Cookie Template + UI Template together, stores multi-locale content, and tracks a version number so you can roll back. Only one profile per domain can be active at a time.
howItWorks.features.consents.title: Consents
howItWorks.features.consents.desc: A searchable, filterable log of every individual consent record submitted by visitors. Each record shows the visitor ID, profile used, locale, GPC signal, consent choices, and timestamp.
howItWorks.features.visitors.title: Visitors
howItWorks.features.visitors.desc: A list of unique visitor identifiers seen across your sites, with their country, first-seen date, and last-seen date. Visitor IDs are pseudonymous UUIDs stored without raw IP addresses.
howItWorks.features.users.title: Users
howItWorks.features.users.desc: Manage the admin accounts that can log into this dashboard. Assign roles, activate or deactivate accounts, and restrict users to specific sites in multi-tenant deployments.
howItWorks.features.roles.title: Roles
howItWorks.features.roles.desc: Define named permission sets (e.g. Viewer, Editor, Admin). Assign granular permissions such as consent:view, profile:edit, or audit:view to each role, then assign roles to users.
howItWorks.features.vendors.title: Vendors
howItWorks.features.vendors.desc: Browse the IAB Global Vendor List (GVL) to look up TCF vendor IDs, declared purposes, and legitimate-interest purposes. Use these IDs when configuring cookie categories in your templates.
howItWorks.features.audit.title: Audit Log
howItWorks.features.audit.desc: An append-only, tamper-evident log of every administrative action — profile saves, user changes, API key creation, and more. Required for GDPR accountability obligations.
howItWorks.features.api.title: API & Keys
howItWorks.features.api.desc: Generate and revoke API tokens, configure allowed CORS origins for the public consent API, and browse the interactive OpenAPI reference to understand every endpoint.
howItWorks.features.settings.title: Settings
howItWorks.features.settings.desc: View your account details, change your password, and export consent or audit data as CSV, XLSX, or JSON for offline analysis or regulatory submission.
howItWorks.docs.heading: Full Documentation
howItWorks.docs.text: Detailed guides, configuration references, integration examples, and a live playground are available on the Consenti documentation site.
howItWorks.consentFlow.heading: End-to-End Consent Flow
howItWorks.consentFlow.step1.title: Widget Initializes
howItWorks.consentFlow.step1.desc: The Consenti script loads in the visitor's browser and reads your configuration (API key, compliance.type). If a valid, unexpired consent cookie already exists, the widget silently exits — no banner is shown.
howItWorks.consentFlow.step2.title: Jurisdiction Detected
howItWorks.consentFlow.step2.desc: If compliance.type is 'auto', the widget calls GET /resolve-profile passing the visitor's timezone, Accept-Language header, and IP address. The server maps these signals to a complianceGroup using the built-in country → compliance map.
howItWorks.consentFlow.step3.title: Profile Fetched
howItWorks.consentFlow.step3.desc: The widget fetches the active profile for the resolved complianceGroup and the visitor's locale. The response is a flat JSON object with all banner text, cookie categories, UI settings, and regulation flags — ready to render.
howItWorks.consentFlow.step4.title: Banner Rendered
howItWorks.consentFlow.step4.desc: The widget renders the banner or preference modal using the profile's UI template. Mandatory categories are pre-checked and cannot be deselected. Optional categories show their legal basis (consent or legitimate interest).
howItWorks.consentFlow.step5.title: Consent Submitted
howItWorks.consentFlow.step5.desc: When the visitor acts (Accept All, Reject All, or Save Preferences), the widget POSTs a consent record to the API with the visitor ID, profile version, per-category status (granted/denied/objected), GPC signal, timestamp, and locale.
howItWorks.consentFlow.step6.title: Stored and Audited
howItWorks.consentFlow.step6.desc: The API validates the payload, stores the consent record, and appends an entry to the append-only audit log. The visitor record is upserted with country and first/last-seen dates. The widget writes the consent cookie so future visits skip the banner.
howItWorks.profileResolution.heading: Profile Resolution Algorithm
howItWorks.profileResolution.intro: When a visitor arrives, Consenti resolves which consent profile to show in five steps.
howItWorks.profileResolution.step1.title: Geo Signals Collected
howItWorks.profileResolution.step1.desc: The widget sends three signals to GET /resolve-profile: the browser's Intl.DateTimeFormat timezone, the Accept-Language header (or lang query param), and the server-side IP address.
howItWorks.profileResolution.step2.title: Country Determined
howItWorks.profileResolution.step2.desc: The server tries in order: (1) IP geolocation via the configured geoDataProvider; (2) timezone → country table lookup; (3) language tag → country heuristic (e.g. 'de' → DE). The first non-empty match wins.
howItWorks.profileResolution.step3.title: Compliance Group Mapped
howItWorks.profileResolution.step3.desc: The ISO-3166-1 alpha-2 country code (plus optional region/state) is looked up in the embedded COUNTRY → COMPLIANCE_GROUP map. A region override (e.g. US/CA → optOutCalifornia) takes precedence over the country default.
howItWorks.profileResolution.step4.title: Active Profile Located
howItWorks.profileResolution.step4.desc: The system queries the database for the active profile whose complianceGroup matches the resolved group. Only one profile per complianceGroup may be active at a time. If none exists, a 404 is returned and the widget may fall back to a profileOverride.
howItWorks.profileResolution.step5.title: Locale Resolved
howItWorks.profileResolution.step5.desc: The best locale is picked by: (1) exact match with the widget's requested locale; (2) language-prefix match (e.g. 'de-AT' → 'de'); (3) geo-resolved locale from the provider; (4) the profile's defaultLocale. The resolved JSON is served from a static file (published profiles) or directly from the database (previews).
howItWorks.complianceGroups.heading: Compliance Groups
howItWorks.complianceGroups.intro: Every country in the world maps to one of eight compliance groups. Each group determines what consent model the widget must follow.
howItWorks.complianceGroups.optIn.title: optIn — GDPR / ePrivacy Strict Opt-In
howItWorks.complianceGroups.optIn.desc: Prior consent is required before any non-essential cookie is set. Visitors must be given a genuine choice to reject, withdrawal must be as easy as giving consent, and granular per-category choices are mandatory.
howItWorks.complianceGroups.optIn.laws: GDPR, ePrivacy Directive, UK GDPR, PECR, revFADP (CH), KVKK (TR), PDPA (TH), and other strict national laws
howItWorks.complianceGroups.optIn.countr: All EU member states, UK, Switzerland, Turkey, Thailand, Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway, Guernsey, Gibraltar, and other countries with strict ePrivacy rules
howItWorks.complianceGroups.optInDPDPA.d: India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 requires explicit opt-in consent. Additional obligations include naming a Data Fiduciary, providing a dedicated consent notice, and supporting Data Principal rights. Set the dpdpaDataFiduciary extra field in your profile.
howItWorks.complianceGroups.optInChina.d: China's data governance requires explicit consent under PIPL, combined with obligations from the Data Security Law (DSL) and Cybersecurity Law (CSL). Additional requirements apply to cross-border data transfers and data classification.
howItWorks.complianceGroups.optInChina.l: Personal Information Protection Law (PIPL), Data Security Law (DSL), Cybersecurity Law (CSL)
howItWorks.complianceGroups.optOutCalifo: Data may be collected by default but consumers must be provided with a clear 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' opt-out. The Global Privacy Control (GPC) browser signal must be honoured as a valid opt-out.
CCPA, CPRA (California Consumer Privacy Act / California Privacy Rights Act)
howItWorks.complianceGroups.optOutUSStat: Modelled on California's opt-out framework. Consumers can opt out of targeted advertising and the sale of personal data. Several states require GPC to be honoured. Covered businesses must provide clear opt-out mechanisms.
CDPA (VA), CPA (CO), CTDPA (CT), UCPA (UT), TDPSA (TX), DPDPA (DE), NJDPA (NJ), OCPA (OR), MCDPA (MN), MODPA (MD), KCDPA (KY), TIPA (TN), NDPA (NE), ICDPA (IA/IN), MCDPA (MT), NHPA (NH), RIDTPA (RI)
United States — VA, CO, CT, UT, TX, DE, NJ, OR, MN, MD, KY, TN, NE, IA, IN, MT, NH, RI
howItWorks.complianceGroups.generalPriva: A general privacy law applies but does not impose a strict PECR/ePrivacy-style cookie banner requirement. Best practice is to offer a consent choice for non-essential cookies and a clear privacy notice, but the legal threshold is lower than GDPR opt-in.
PIPEDA / Law 25 (CA), LGPD (BR — use hybridBrazil), POPIA (ZA), APPI (JP), Privacy Act (AU/NZ), PDPA-SG, PDPA-MY, PIPA-KR, PDPD-VN, DPA-PH, PDP-ID and many others
Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Japan, South Korea, Singapore, Malaysia, South Africa, and many LatAm, African, and Asian countries
howItWorks.complianceGroups.hybridBrazil: Brazil's LGPD is broadly similar to GDPR but analytics and advertising cookies may be treated under the legitimate interest legal basis depending on the data controller's assessment. The banner is shown, but the category-level legal basis can be more nuanced than strict GDPR opt-in.
Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados (LGPD)
howItWorks.complianceGroups.noticeOnly.d: No dedicated cookie regulation applies, or the existing regime requires only a privacy notice — not an opt-in or opt-out banner. Displaying a consent banner is still recommended for transparency, but is not strictly mandated by law.
howItWorks.complianceGroups.noticeOnly.l: Varies — countries with no dedicated cookie / ePrivacy regulation
howItWorks.complianceGroups.noticeOnly.c: Many countries in Africa, Central Asia, the Caribbean, and Pacific regions not covered by the above groups
howItWorks.enums.heading: Key Values Reference
howItWorks.enums.legalBasis.heading: legalBasis (cookie category)
howItWorks.enums.legalBasis.mandatory: mandatory — Category is always active regardless of consent (e.g. session, authentication). Never blocked by GPC or opt-out signals.
howItWorks.enums.legalBasis.consent: consent — Requires explicit opt-in from the visitor. Blocked by default; blocked by GPC in all opt-in jurisdictions.
howItWorks.enums.legalBasis.legitimateIn: legitimate_interest — Relies on the controller's legitimate interest. Active by default; visitor can object. Soft-blocked by GPC in some jurisdictions (EU, UK).
howItWorks.enums.consentStatus.heading: ConsentStatus (per category record)
howItWorks.enums.consentStatus.granted: granted — Visitor gave explicit consent for this category.
howItWorks.enums.consentStatus.denied: denied — Visitor explicitly refused consent for this category.
howItWorks.enums.consentStatus.objected: objected — Visitor objected to a legitimate-interest category (opt-out, not a refusal of consent).
howItWorks.enums.geoProvider.heading: geoDataProvider options
howItWorks.enums.geoProvider.default: default / timezone — Uses the browser timezone (Intl.DateTimeFormat) to infer country, falling back to Accept-Language. No extra dependency.
howItWorks.enums.geoProvider.language: language — Derives country from Accept-Language only (e.g. 'de' → DE, 'pt' → BR). Fast but imprecise.
howItWorks.enums.geoProvider.geoip: geoip — Uses the geoip-lite npm package with a bundled MaxMind Lite database. Requires: npm install geoip-lite.
howItWorks.enums.geoProvider.hostedGeoip: hosted-geoip-lite — Calls ipinfo.io via built-in HTTPS (zero extra dependency). Adds ~50–200 ms per first-visit request.
howItWorks.enums.geoProvider.maxmind: maxmind — Uses the maxmind npm package with a local GeoLite2-City.mmdb file. Most accurate. Requires MAXMIND_DB_PATH env var and npm install maxmind.
howItWorks.enums.geoProvider.custom: custom function — Pass a CountryResolverFn to compliance.geoDataProvider to integrate any geo-IP or CDN provider.
